coredata.dc:title: Smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on NS-codoped carbon dots/Au nanoclusters for rapid detection of Hg2+
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jieyuan
Wang, Ting
Du, Yuxuan
Wu, Yue
Jiang, Xinhui
Zou, Hecun
Zhang, Zhengwei
coredata.dc:description: Mercury metal can be absorbed into human body through the food chain, and cause considerable harm to human health. Herein, a novel smartphone-enabled ratiometric fluorescence sensing system, which was made up of nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) and Au nanoclusters (AuNCs), had developed for rapid detection of mercury ion (Hg2+). The NSCDs/AuNCs displayed dual-emission fluorescence at 406/690 nm. After introducing Hg2+, the fluorescence at 690 nm was obviously quenched, while the signal around 406 nm was slightly attenuated, causing a color change from pink-purple to blue. Under the most suitable conditions, the ratiometric fluorescence probe had two linear ranges of 0.05–3.0 and 3.0–7.0 μM with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 18 nM. The approach was utilized to detect Hg2+ in real water samples. Furthermore, a portable smartphone-assisted sensing platform based on NSCDs/AuNCs provides a simple, affordable, and efficient method for monitoring Hg2+ in the environments and other fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NSCDs
AuNCs
Mercury ion
Ratiometric fluorescence
Smartphone sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real samples
The characterizations of NSCDs, AuNCs, and NSCDs/AuNCs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: CQMU Program for Youth Innovation in Future Medicine
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing Municipality

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 82003713 and 82003854 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (cstc2020jcyj-msxmX0158 and cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0302), and CQMU Program for Youth Innovation in Future Medicine (W0152).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A–C) TEM images of NSCDs, AuNCs, and NSCDs/AuNCs (from left to right). (D) Zeta potentials of NSCDs, AuNCs, and NSCDs/AuNCs. (E) UV–vis absorption spectra of NSCDs, AuNCs, and NSCDs/AuNCs. (F) FL spectra of NSCDs, AuNCs, and NSCDs/AuNCs under 330 nm excitation. Insert: digital pictures of NSCDs, AuNCs, and NSCDs/AuNCs under a 302 nm UV lamp (from left to right).
FL spectra of NSCDs (A), AuNCs (B), and NSCDs/AuNCs (C) under different excitation wavelengths.
XPS survey (A) and peak fitting scans of C 1s (B), N 1s (C), O 1s (D), and S 2p (E) regions for NSCDs. (F) FT–IR spectra of NSCDs, AuNCs, and NSCDs/AuNCs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of NSCDs/AuNCs after adding various concentrations of Hg2+ (0–7.0 μM). (B) Correlation curves between (F0-F)690/F406 and Hg2+ concentration over the range of 0.05–7.0 μM. (C) CIE plot after adding different concentrations of Hg2+ (0–7.0 μM). (D) The selectivity of NSCDs/AuNCs probe toward Hg2+. Error bar represents the standard deviation (n = 3).
RGB analysis for Hg2+ detection using color recognizer App on the smartphone.
Schematic diagram of the synthetic process of NSCDs/AuNCs and selective sensing for Hg2+.
Results for the determination of Hg2+ in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on NS-codoped carbon dots/Au nanoclusters for rapid detection of Hg2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 400016, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmacy, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 400016, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Life Sciences, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 400016, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Life Sciences, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 400016, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, The First Affiliated Hospital of Chongqing Medical and Pharmaceutical College, Chongqing 400060, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First Affiliated Hospital of Chongqing Medical and Pharmaceutical College

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacy, The First Affiliated Hospital of Chongqing Medical and Pharmaceutical College, Chongqing 400060, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mercury metal can be absorbed into human body through the food chain, and cause considerable harm to human health. Herein, a novel smartphone-enabled ratiometric fluorescence sensing system, which was made up of nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) and Au nanoclusters (AuNCs), had developed for rapid detection of mercury ion (Hg2+). The NSCDs/AuNCs displayed dual-emission fluorescence at 406/690 nm. After introducing Hg2+, the fluorescence at 690 nm was obviously quenched, while the signal around 406 nm was slightly attenuated, causing a color change from pink-purple to blue. Under the most suitable conditions, the ratiometric fluorescence probe had two linear ranges of 0.05–3.0 and 3.0–7.0 μM with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 18 nM. The approach was utilized to detect Hg2+ in real water samples. Furthermore, a portable smartphone-assisted sensing platform based on NSCDs/AuNCs provides a simple, affordable, and efficient method for monitoring Hg2+ in the environments and other fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, heavy metal contamination has attracted growing concern owing to its detrimental impact on the ecological environment and the well-being of humans [1]. Mercury ion (Hg2+) is one of the most common heavy metals that found in today's environmental waterways [2]. While Hg2+ can be absorbed into human body through the food chain, and cause considerable harm to human health, even tiny accumulations would induce the damage of kidney, gastrointestinal, and central nervous system damage including reversible proteinuria, acute tubular necrosis, and nephrotic syndrome [3,4]. To avoid irreparable harm to human health, the content of Hg2+ within food and water samples should be controlled less than the maximum permitted quantity, which is approximately 1.0 mg kg−1 [5]. Therefore, it is critically necessary to develop a focused, sensitive, and real-time sensing platform for the monitoring of Hg2+ in ambient water.

Although some analytical techniques have been used for the qualitative and quantitative detection of Hg2+, including atomic absorption spectroscopy [6], atomic fluorescence spectroscopy [7], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8,9], electrochemical detection [10,11], and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MC) [12]. However, these methods are time-consuming, necessitate expensive instruments, and may not be suitable for real-time detection of Hg2+. Interestingly, fluorescent probes have gradually become a hot topic for the detection of environmental contaminants and biomarkers, due to their benefits of facile synthesis, customizable selectivity, and high sensitivity [13,14]. Recently, the emerging research materials have provided inspiration for a series of fluorescent nanoprobes, such as quantum dots [15,16], metal nanoclusters [17], and metal–organic frameworks [18]. Since these fluorescent methods are typically based on variations of emission intensity at a single wavelength, it leads to a limitation in the sensitivity and resolution of the sensors used for Hg2+ detection.

The design and synthesis of dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence nanosensors pose certain challenges. Carbon dots (CDs) have been employed extensively in biosensing, bioimaging, and catalysis because of their superior optical characteristics, low toxicity, and ease of availability and modification [19–22]. Currently, the ratiometric fluorescence sensors based on CDs have attracted widespread due to their excellent water solubility, good biocompatibility, and stable optical signal. For example, Zheng et al fabricated a dual-emission carbon dots (BYCDs) through a one-pothydrothermal route, which could be utilized as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for sensitive and specific detection of Hg2+ [23]. Guo et al encapsulated fluorescent CDs into Eu-MOFs and conducted a novel ratiometric fluorescence probe (CDs@Eu-MOFs) to detect Hg2+ in environmental water samples [24].

In this study, nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) were rapidly prepared by a one-step microwave process and simply mixed with Au nanoclusters (AuNCs) to construct a ratiometric fluorescence probe (NSCDs@AuNCs), which displayed a high specific recognition ability for Hg2+ (Scheme 1 ). The fluorescence intensity of AuNCs at 690 nm reduced as the quantity of Hg2+ in the samples rose, while the fluorescence strength of NSCDs at 406 nm remained almost unaffected, causing the fluorescence hue to shift from pinkish purple to blue. Furthermore, combined with the smartphone's RGB analysis, the hue changes of the solution were recorded in real-time by the device and converted into RGB values that could be easily discerned with the naked eye. Consequently, we constructed a simple, green and reliable ratiometric fluorescence sensor that provided a powerful tool for sensitive and visual detection of Hg2+.

Thiourea and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from BBI Life Science Co., Ltd. Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), ascorbic acid (AA), D-glutamic acid (D-Glu), arginine (Arg), and valine (Val) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hg(NO3)2, MgSO4, FeCl3, ZnSO4, and AlCl3·6H2O were brought from Chron Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Dialysis membrane (MWCO = 500 ∼ 1000 Da) was purchased from Ruida Henghui Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All reagents were analytical grade without further purification and ultra-pure water was used throughout the experiment.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) pictures were captured using a Tecnai F20 S-Twin TEM (FEI, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT–IR) was recorded on a Nicolet IS10 FT–IR spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo, USA) was utilized to gain the data for the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured through a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence (FL) spectra were obtained by a Edingburgh FS5 spectrofluorometer (Edingburgh, UK). Fluorescence images were acquired from a ZF-20A Black-Box TypeUV analyzer (Shanghai Lingke Industrial Development Co., Ltd, Shanghai). Zeta potential measurements were available through a Zetasizer nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK).

The NSCDs were prepared using microwave-assisted heating method by modifying the reference method [25]. Briefly, 0.88 g of AA and 0.38 g of thiourea were fully dissolved with ultra-pure water, and then heated in an experimental microwave for 10 min on medium heat. The crude product was added to 50 mL of ultra-pure water for solubilization. The NSCDs solution was collected by centrifugation at 14000 rpm for 20 min to get rid of insoluble impurities. To eliminate any leftover precursor, the resultant dark brown solution was dialyzed for 24 h. Finally, the dialysis fluid was lyophilized into brown powder and kept in a desiccator for future use.

The AuNCs were synthesized according to the previous literature [26]. Briefly, 5 mL of HAuCl4 (10 mM) was added to 5 mL of BSA (50 mg/mL) with stirring constantly in 37 °C for 2 min, then 250 mL of the solution containing 1 M of NaOH was introduced, and the combined product was incubated at 37 °C for 12 h. The solution's color changed from pale yellow to orange-red, indicating that the AuNCs were successfully prepared. The obtained suspension was kept at 4 °C refrigerator for further use.

In a standard Hg2+ detection experiment, 100 μL of NSCDs (1 mg/mL) and 600 μL of AuNCs were added to BR buffer (40 mM H3PO4, 40 mM HAc, and 40 mM H3BO3, adjusting the pH to 8.0 with 0.2 M of NaOH) to formulate 2 mL of the sensing solution. Then, a range of Hg2+ (0.05–7 μM) were added into the above solution and mixed thoroughly, and after incubation for 5 min, the fluorescence intensities of NSCDs/AuNCs were collected under a 330 nm excitation wavelength at room temperature. For the investigation of selectivity, some amino acids (Cys, D-Glu, L-Arg, and Val) and other related ions (Fe3+, Al3+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, and Cr3+) were added to the sensing system to achieve a concentration of 2.5 μM, and the fluorescence tests were performed under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For confirming the possible use of the produced sensor, actual samples including river and tap water were chosen. Water samples were filtered through a membrane filter of 0.22 μm. The filtered water was used to create the spiked samples, which were designed at three concentration points of 1, 3, and 6 μM. The fluorescence detection process was the same as the above steps.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterizations of NSCDs, AuNCs, and NSCDs/AuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size of NSCDs, AuNCs, and NSCDs/AuNCs were examined by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A and B, both NSCDs and AuNCs were well mono-dispersive and spherical, with average sizes of approximately 2 and 3 nm, respectively. The Fig. 1C depicted the bigger particle size of NSCDs/AuNCs, confirming the formation of the NSCDs-AuNCs complex [27]. The zeta potentials of NSCDs and AuNCs were −1.93 ± 0.30 and 12.1 ± 1.15 mV (Fig. 1D), respectively, indicating that they can bind well through electrostatic interactions. The Fig. 1E revealed the UV–vis absorption characteristics of NSCDs, AuNCs, and NSCDs/AuNCs. The NSCDs had a high absorption peak at 255 nm that can be credited for π-π* transition of aromatic sp2-hybridized carbon [28]. Two significant absorption peaks could be observed at 230 and 275 nm for AuNCs. However, the NSCDs/AuNCs exhibited a distinct peak at 223 nm and a broad absorption bond from 245 to 300 nm. The respective fluorescence spectra of NSCDs, AuNCs, and NSCDs/AuNCs were obtained under 330 nm excitation (Fig. 1F). The NSCDs and AuNCs showed strong emissions at 406 and 690 nm, respectively, while dual-emission fluorescence can be seen from NSCDs/AuNCs. When placed under a 302 nm UV lamp, the NSCDs and AuNCs displayed blue and red fluorescence, respectively, while the NSCDs/AuNCs were pinkish-purple.

The results of fluorescence emission spectra of NSCDs, AuNCs, and NSCDs/AuNCs were as follows. The NSCDs exhibited the highest emission intensity at 406 nm under an excitation wavelength of 330 nm (Fig. 2 A). Since the size of CDs impacts the light selectivity, the NSCDs exhibit excitation wavelength-dependence at various excitation wavelengths, and the emission bands shift toward the red with increasing excitation wavelength [29]. The emission spectra of AuNCs were shown in the Fig. 2B. As the excitation wavelength varied from 300 to 430 nm, the AuNCs displayed the excitation wavelength‐independent fluorescence character, and the maximum excitation wavelength was 350 nm. Additionally, the fluorescent characteristics of NSCDs/AuNCs were consistent with those of NSCDs and AuNCs (Fig. 2C). The optimal dual-emission fluorescence could be obtained under a 330 nm excitation wavelength, which was selected as the ideal excitation wavelength for the sensing experiments. The pH of the medium has an impact on the surface functional states of nanomaterials, which might further alter their fluorescence intensities [30]. The effect of pH on the stability of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor was investigated (Fig. S1). The results showed that when the pH was less than 7, the sensing system was damaged due to the loss of the fluorescence of NSCDs. When the pH was in the range of 8–9, the NSCDs/AuNCs exhibited outstanding dual-emission behavior, confirming that the alkaline system is more suitable for subsequent sensing research.

The composition and elemental chemical states of NSCDs were analyzed by XPS. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the NSCDs had four different bond energy peaks at 284.8, 399.3, 531.7, and 163.6 eV, respectively, corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and S 2p, respectively, indicating nitrogen and sulfur elements were successfully doped into the CDs [31]. In the high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3B), three energy peaks at 284.8, 286.24, and 288.25 eV originated from the C = C, C-N/C-O, and C = O bonds, respectively [32]. The high-resolution energy spectra of N 1s could be fitted to three peaks at 399.34, 400.09, and 400.86 eV, which were attributed to C-N-C, N-C, and N-H (Fig. 3C) [33]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum displayed two peaks for the C-O-C/ C-OH and C = O groups at 531.77 and 532.89 eV (Fig. 3D) [34]. The three peaks at 161.87, 163.77, and 168.44 eV, corresponding to S 2p3/2, S 2p1/2, and S = O, respectively, were used to partition the high-resolution energy spectrum of S 2p (Fig. 3E) [35].

The surface functional groups of NSCDs, AuNCs, and NSCDs/AuNCs were implemented by FT–IR spectroscopy (Fig. 3F). The NSCDs had a broad peak around 3210 cm−1, belonging to the O–H and N–H stretching vibration [36]. While the peak at 1660 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of the C = C group, the acute absorption peak at 1410 cm−1 related to the stretching oscillation of the C-N group. The main contributors to a small absorption peak at 1100 cm−1 were C-S, C-O, and C-N bonds [37,38]. The broad band peak at 693 cm−1 was generated by -SO3H. It showed that the AuNCs had no significant surface plasmon resonance absorption peak at 520 cm−1, demonstrating the formation of gold nanoclusters rather than gold nanoparticles. The FT–IR spectrum of AuNCs was mainly controlled by the bands associated with the stretching vibrations of O-H at 3744 cm−1, O-H and N-H at 3213 cm−1, C-H at 2950 cm−1, C = O at 1650 cm−1, and C-O at 1070 cm−1 [39]. Additionally, the FT–IR spectrum of NSCDs/AuNCs matched the respective spectra of NSCDs and AuNCs, confirming the successful construction of fluorescent composite sensor.

After adding Hg2+ to the NSCDs/AuNCs system, the fluorescence at 690 nm was clearly quenched, but the signal at 406 nm was weakly diminished, indicating that the ratiometric fluorescence method for Hg2+detection could be constructed (Fig. S2). Based on previous reports [40,41], the sensing mechanism may be attributed to the high-affinity metallophilic Hg2+-Au+ interactions, which can effectively quench the fluorescence of AuNCs. Subsequently, the reaction conditions were optimized to provide the best analytical results [42,43]. The Fig. S3A presented the effect of different volume ratios of NSCDs to AuNCs on the fluorescence efficiency of Hg2+ bursting. It demonstrated that when the volume ratio of NSCDs to AuNCs was 1:6, it had the best bursting effect. So that the volume ratio of 1:6 was chosen as the optimal concentration ratio for subsequent studies. To examine the impact of buffer systems on the fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F0-F)/F0) and initial fluorescence intensity (F0) of the sensor, several buffer systems were utilized as the solvents (Fig. S3B). It showed that the (F0-F)/F0 of the sensor was affected by various buffer systems, but only BR buffer had the highest initial fluorescence. Thus, BR buffer was chosen as the solvent for the future trials, and would allow the sensor to detect Hg2+ across a larger range. Then, the impact of incubation temperature on the (F0-F)/F0 of the sensor was studied (Fig. S3C). The results indicated that the (F0-F)/F0 was good at an incubation temperature of 20 °C, and Hg2+ could fully interact with the sensor. In addition, the (F0-F)/F0 of the sensor changed minimally with incubation times ranging from 5 to 40 min (Fig. S3D). Consequently, an incubation time of 5 min was chosen for the latter tests.

To assess the detection efficiency of the nanocomposite probe for Hg2+, the quenching effect of varying Hg2+ concentrations on the sensor was tested under the optimum experimental conditions stated above. As shown in Fig. 4 A, as the quantity of Hg2+ gradually increased (0.05–7 μM), the nanoprobe's fluorescence at 690 nm progressively decreased, while the fluorescence signal at 406 nm slightly changed. There were two good linear relationships between (F0-F)690/F406 and Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0.05–3.0 and 3.0–7.0 μM, respectively. The fitted linear equations were (F0-F)690/F406 = 12.0177C + 6.0701 (R2 = 0.9946) and (F0-F)690/F406 = 8.8072C + 13.9592 (R2 = 0.9969), respectively (Fig. 4B). The limit of detection (LOD) of Hg2+ was calculated to be 18 nM (S/N = 3) [44]. As listed in Table S1, comparation to other detection methods reported previously, the NSCDs/AuNCs sensor had a broader linear range and a lower LOD toward Hg2+. The CIE chromaticity diagram showed a significant color change from pink-purple to blue, demonstrating the usefulness of Hg2+ visual evaluation (Fig. 4C). These results indicated the NSCDs/AuNCs probe for assaying Hg2+ had overwhelmingly feasible merits like simplicity and practicality.

To investigate the selectivity of the NSCDs/AuNCs probe, certain foreign substances, such as metal ions and amino acids, that might coexist in the sensing system were tested. As shown in Fig. 4D, the highest quenching effect (690 nm) was observed after intruding Hg2+, while other interfering substances did not have a notable influence on the FL intensity of NSCDs/AuNCs. These results indicated that the NSCDs/AuNCs hold high selectivity for detecting Hg2+.

The analysis method of Hg2+ in real water samples was established and its reliability was evaluated in Table 1 . The recoveries of the spiked samples fell within the range of 95.33 %–106.3 %, with an RSD of ≤ 5.14 %. These results demonstrated the practicability and reliability of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor for detecting Hg2+ in real water samples.

As presented in Fig. 5 , the fluorescent color photos were taken by a smartphone under a 302 nm UV lamp in the dark box. With the increase of the Hg2+ concentration, the color of dual-emission fluorescence sensor gradually changed from pinkish purple to lighter and then to blue, demonstrating the potential of the sensor in visual quantification of Hg2+ [45]. The colors in the pictures were transformed to RGB values by the smartphone's color detector. Red (R) and blue (B) colors were used to determine the RGB ratio because the fluorescent hue of the sensor progressively transitions from red to blue. The RGB value (B/R) of fluorescent sensor and the Hg2+concentration possessed a well linear relationship ranging from 2 to 9 µM, with the linear equation: B/R = 0.2061C + 0.4058 (R2 = 0.9879). In conclusion, visual statistical tracking of Hg2+ was made possible by the suggested sensor device in conjunction with a smartphone App.

In summary, the NSCDs/AuNCs were fabricated and utilized as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for rapid and visual detection of Hg2+. The prepared NSCDs/AuNCs exhibited dual-emission fluorescence at 406 and 690 nm, and presented color changes in the fluorescence signals after introducing Hg2+, which considerably improved the selectivity and accuracy of the sensing system. Hg2+ was detected in a wide concentration range of 0.05–7.0 μg/mL, with a detection limit as low as 18 nM. The remarkable selectivity of the probe towards Hg2+ was confirmed through interference studies on various metal ions and amino acids. In the actual sample analysis, the spiked recoveries were 95.33 %–106.3 %, with an RSD of ≤ 5.14 %, demonstrating that the sensor had a certain degree of accuracy and precision. More importantly, a smartphone-assisted sensing platform was conducted to provide a fast and convenient method for the visualization and quantitative detection of Hg2+, which had enormous potential in metal ion analysis. This work afforded a promising platform for visual detection of Hg2+ in the environments and other fields.

Jieyuan Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ting Wang: Writing – original draft, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yuxuan Du: Software, Investigation, Data curation. Yue Wu: Software, Methodology, Investigation. Xinhui Jiang: Investigation, Methodology. Hecun Zou: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Zhengwei Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82003713 and 82003854), the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (cstc2020jcyj-msxmX0158 and cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0302), and CQMU Program for Youth Innovation in Future Medicine (W0152).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110201.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
